title: Parental leave

meta.description: Parental leave entitlements in Australia — unpaid leave, eligibility, how to apply, and right to return.

content:
Parental leave gives employees time off work for the birth or adoption of a child. There are NES entitlements to unpaid parental leave, and a separate government-funded Paid Parental Leave scheme.

Eligibility for unpaid parental leave:
All employees (full-time, part-time, and some casual employees) are eligible for unpaid parental leave if they have completed at least 12 months of continuous service with their employer before the expected date of birth or adoption.

Casual employees are eligible if they have been employed on a regular and systematic basis for at least 12 months and have a reasonable expectation of continuing employment.

Types of parental leave:
1. Unpaid parental leave (NES entitlement)
2. Government-funded Paid Parental Leave (Services Australia — separate scheme)
3. Paid parental leave under an award or enterprise agreement (additional entitlements)

Unpaid parental leave:
- Eligible employees can take up to 12 months of unpaid parental leave
- Employees can request an additional 12 months (up to 24 months total)
- The employer can only refuse the extension on reasonable business grounds
- Both parents cannot take parental leave at the same time generally, except for a short concurrent period (up to 8 weeks at the same time)

Leave can be taken:
- For birth: up to 12 months from the birth of the child
- For adoption: up to 12 months from the day of placement

Paid Parental Leave (government scheme):
- Funded by the Australian Government through Services Australia (not the employer)
- Eligible working parents may receive government-funded PPL payments
- This is separate from the NES unpaid parental leave entitlement
- The employer does not pay PPL unless their award or agreement provides additional entitlements

Right to return to work:
After parental leave, an employee has the right to return to:
- Their pre-parental leave position, or
- If that role no longer exists, a comparable position (same pay, location, status)

An employer cannot dismiss an employee because they are on parental leave or because they have applied for parental leave. This is protected by the Fair Work Act.

Applying for parental leave:
Employees must give their employer at least 10 weeks' written notice before taking parental leave (or as soon as reasonably practicable if the birth or adoption is earlier than expected). The notice must include the expected start date and duration of leave.
